coredata.dc:title: Ion coordination and chelation in Eu-MOFs matrices: Ultrafast fluorescence visual quantification monitoring of antibiotic residues
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yujie
Li, Lingfei
Xu, Shihao
Zhang, Maofeng
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: Rapid monitoring of trace antibiotics in the field in real time is essential for environment forewarning and human health. High sensitivity and real-time on-site quantitative monitoring of antibiotic residues can be accomplished by integrating portable sensors alongside fluorescent optics to construct an intelligent sensing platform that smoothly eliminates the instability of conventional detection methods. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of pefloxacin was built employing the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism from red Eu-MOFs to Mn2+-PEF complex. A visual color change results from the photoinduced electron transfer process from manganese ions to pefloxacin weakening the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) process in Eu-MOFs. This enables the ultrafast visible detection of pefloxacin and produces a transient shift in visual color with a detection limit as low as 15.4 nM. For the detection of pefloxacin in water, tomato, and raw pork samples, various sensing devices based on the developed fluorescent probes exhibit good practicability and accuracy. With the development of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing probe, it is now possible to quickly and quantitatively identify pefloxacin residues in the environment, offering a new method for ensuring the safety of food and people's health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ion coordination
Eu-MOFs
Visual quantification monitoring
Antibiotic residues

Detecting PEF on a smartphone platform
Detection of PEF in real samples
The sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Construction and optimization of ratiometric fluorescent probe
Detection of PEF in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (a), (b) and HRTEM image (c) of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs. TEM images (d), (e) and HRTEM image (f) of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF. EDX elemental mapping (g) of coordination environments of the O atoms (h), Eu atoms (i) and Mn atoms (j) in the building unit of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs.

UV–vis spectra (A) and IR spectrum (B) of PEF, Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF. (C) Schematic diagram of emission levels of Eu-MOFs and Mn2+-PEF and energy transfer from Mn2+/Eu-MOFs to PEF (The inset shows the frontier molecular orbitals of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF). (D) The Zeta potential diagram of each component in the detection system.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe solution upon adding different concentrations of PEF (The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence photos under 254 nm UV irradiation). (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio I446/I616 and PEF concentration. (C) CIE chromaticity variation diagram of probe solution. (D) Relationship between probe fluorescence intensity ratio I446/I616 and reaction time (The inset shows fluorescence photos of probe solution corresponding to different reaction times after adding 12 μM PEF under 254 nm UV irradiation). (E) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I446/I616) of the probe upon the addition of 12 μM of various interfering substances (The inset at the side shows the corresponding fluorescence color photos under 254 nm UV irradiation). (F) Selectivity and anti-interference test of the probe (The black bar represents the interference substance test, and the red bar represents the PEF + interference substance test, where All is the PEF+ All interference substance test).

(A) Schematic diagram of a hand-held fluorescent needle. (B) Color change when a fluorescent needle detects PEF (0–12 μM). (C) Schematic diagram of the PEF detection system of a smartphone. (D) B/R ratio of the fluorescence probe color shift with PEF concentration, ranging from 0 to 12 μM.
PEF diagram of porous plate detection and its application in real sample detection.
The illustration of the Eu-MOFs based ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for visual detection of PEF.
Marking detection of PEF in lake water, tap water, tomato juice and raw pork with a ratiometric fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Ion coordination and chelation in Eu-MOFs matrices: Ultrafast fluorescence visual quantification monitoring of antibiotic residues

Corresponding author. Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui, 230031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid monitoring of trace antibiotics in the field in real time is essential for environment forewarning and human health. High sensitivity and real-time on-site quantitative monitoring of antibiotic residues can be accomplished by integrating portable sensors alongside fluorescent optics to construct an intelligent sensing platform that smoothly eliminates the instability of conventional detection methods. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of pefloxacin was built employing the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism from red Eu-MOFs to Mn2+-PEF complex. A visual color change results from the photoinduced electron transfer process from manganese ions to pefloxacin weakening the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) process in Eu-MOFs. This enables the ultrafast visible detection of pefloxacin and produces a transient shift in visual color with a detection limit as low as 15.4 nM. For the detection of pefloxacin in water, tomato, and raw pork samples, various sensing devices based on the developed fluorescent probes exhibit good practicability and accuracy. With the development of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing probe, it is now possible to quickly and quantitatively identify pefloxacin residues in the environment, offering a new method for ensuring the safety of food and people's health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pefloxacin (PEF), a third-generation quinolone antibiotic, is a commonly used broad-spectrum antibiotic for the treatment of bacterial infectious diseases [1]. However, it's usually going away after use in the form of parent compounds or metabolites or is deposited directly in organisms due to its good water solubility and stability. This allows it to accumulate and spread in the ecosystem and poses a serious threat to human health and the ecological environment [2–4]. As a result, there is a growing demand for the development and implementation of an accurate and reliable method for detecting antibiotics, which is crucial for safeguarding the environment and enhancing human health. Currently, microbiological approach [5], immunoassay [6], high performance liquid chromatography [7], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [8,9], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy are the primary methods for detecting fluoroquinolone residues [10]. The aforementioned methods, however, generally come with drawbacks such costly equipment, difficult sample pretreatment, time-consuming, and low specificity, and they mostly require laboratory execution, which is not favorable to visual quantitative field detection [11–13].

Fluorescence sensing technology has attracted a lot of attention increasingly for the reason to its benefits, which include visibility, ease of use, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. The substantial amount of active sites and expansive surface modification of functionalized fluorescent nanomaterials have rendered them a desirable option for analyte assessment [14,15]. Carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots (QDs), and metal-organic skeletons constitute a few of the fluorescent nanomaterials that have been created thus far. These items cover a range of visible light emission, from monochrome to multicolor [16–22]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing, as opposed to monochromatic fluorescence sensing, uses two or more distinct emissions for generating an internal reference, which may minimize background interference and facilitate quantitative analyte detection [23,24]. Whereas traditional fluorescence sensors such quantum dots and carbon dots frequently provide benefits like resilient stability, straightforward preparation, and adjustable structure, there currently is still an obstacle with their toxicity [25–27]. In addition, the usage is restricted by their very broader emission peaks, lack of distinct recognition sites, and challenging surface modification [28–31]. The metal-organic skeleton materials employed in luminescence, however, successfully tackle these issues.

In view of the distinctive structure and designability of MOFs, luminescence MOFs (LMOFs) offer a variety of customizable emission sources, which include metal ions, ligands, and guest molecules. Their companies also have an assortment of luminescence characteristics and function well for biological imaging and fluorescence visualization detection [32–35]. Among these instances, the lanthanide metal-organic skeleton (Ln-MOFs) with lanthanide metal ions (Ln3+) as metal nodes synthesizes the special benefits of MOFs and the inborn qualities of lanthanide ions, and is a significant component of LMOFs [36–38]. Trivalent lanthanide rare earth ions have a typical ground-state electron configuration [Xe] 4fn (n = 0–14) and are capable of producing f-f emission in the spectral region from ultraviolet (Gd3+) to visible light (e.g. Eu3+, Tb3+) to near-infrared (e.g. Er3+, Yb3+) [39–41]. At the same time, due to the shielding effect of the outer 5s25p6, lanthanide ions (except Ce3+) exhibit a clear, narrow and unique emission spectrum through the 4f-4f transition, and the electron transition in their 4f orbital is almost free from interference from the external environment [42–44]. In addition, the inhibited nature of the 4f-4f transition slows down the relaxation of the excited state of Ln3+, so that Ln3+ usually exhibits a long luminescent lifetime of up to microseconds or even milliseconds [45,46]. In view of this, Ln-MOFs have so been utilized in an assortment of antibiotic identification methods [47–50].

With superior accuracy and sensitivity, cheaper cost, and design downsizing than conventional industrial technology, portable intelligent detection equipment can fulfill the demands of visual quantitative field detection of various residues, and it may additionally accomplish quantitative identification. Based on this, we constructed a multi-emission Mn2+/Eu-MOFs with a single wavelength that utilizes the ions of Mn2+, 3, 5-dicarboxyphenyl boric acid, and europium for visual monitoring of pefloxacin with a detection limit as low as 15.4 nM. Upon the addition of pefloxacin, the visible emission color shifts from red to blue, and the emission peak of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs progressively diminishes at 616 nm and gradually increases at 446 nm. By mounting the fluorescent probe in a portable detector needle and constructing a smart sensing platform integrated with a mobile phone, it is achievable to detect antibiotics in water, tamaoto juice, and pork in real-time quantitative fashion. This sensing technique facilitates the high sensitivity and visual quantitative monitoring of pefloxacin, and it also broadens the potential occupies of fluorescent MOFs for the detection of environmental pollutants and provides a new way of guaranteeing the safety of food and human health.

3,5-dicarboxyphenyl boric acid (BBDC), europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3 6H2O), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Pefloxacin (PEF), norfloxacin (NFX), enrofloxacin (ENO), ofloxacin (OFL), sparfloxacin (SPX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), oxytet-racycline (OTC), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), genamicin sulfate (GEN), amoxicillin (AMX), tetracycline (TCY), chloramphenicol (CPL), Vitamin B1 (VB1), Vitamin B2 (VB2), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine (Lys K), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2 4H2O), PbCl2, CdCl2 and HgCl2 were all purchased from Sinopol Holding Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (China). Raw pork and tomato come from the local market. Lake samples were taken from Chaohu Lake. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was produced using a Millipore water purification system.

A Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer was utilized to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. An ESCALAB 250 high-performance electron spectrometer from Thermo Scientific has been employed to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies. Utilizing a PerkinElmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer, fluorescence spectra were acquired. Using a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired. Using a Philips X'Pert Pro powder X-ray diffractometer (Netherlands), the X-ray diffraction pattern was acquired. A JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope was utilized for the TEM. Each fluorescence picture has been taken using a Canon EOS600D digital camera at a wavelength of 254 nm utilizing a UVC portable ultraviolet lamp.

EuCl3 6H2O. (0.2 mmol) and BBDC (0.1 mmol) were progressively dissolved in a 7:3 vol ratio DMF/H2O solution. After 15 min of vigorous stirring, they were put into an autoclave lined with Teflon and heated to 120 °C for 12 h to complete the reaction. The product was cooled at room temperature, thoroughly cleaned and centrifuged with DMF and anhydrous ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to obtain Eu-MOFs white powder. The produced Eu-MOFs (50 mg) was dissolved uniformly in the solution of MnCl2 4H2O (30 ml, 0.5 mM), and stirred for 12 h at room temperature. After drying and centrifuging the solution, Mn2+/Eu-MOFs probe powder was produced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacturing the ratiometric fluorescent probe for PEF detection
ce:section.ce:para: To produce a homogenous probe solution with a concentration of 0.025 mg/ml, a certain quantity of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs powder was ultrasonically disseminated in deionized water. After thoroughly shaking, the reaction solution was created by combining the probe solution (2 mL) with PEF solution (10 μL) at various concentrations. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to record the proportional fluorescence probe's fluorescence spectra at a single wavelength of 254 nm after 5 min of reaction, and a UV lamp was used to observe color changes. Each experiment was averaged after three measurements. In addition, a total of 14 antibiotics and heavy metal ions were utilized to assess the selectivity and anti-interference of the sensor system at 254 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting PEF on a smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: The initial fluorescence intensity ratio (I446/I616) of the probe solution was 0.13/1, and the color shifted from red to blue was observed when different concentrations of PEF were added, and the fluorescence intensity ratio likewise rose. Under 254 nm UV light, a smartphone was used to take a proportionate fluorescent color photo of the probe solution. The RGB value of the color was then collected using the Han Wanjie smartphone universal color picker program. To confirm the correctness of the outcome, the average B/R value was calculated for each image using three randomly chosen locations.

Below is the construction process for a portable, hand-held fluorescent needle. The luminous needle's needle, cylinder, piston, tail nut, and screw push rod are all meticulously cleaned, dried, and assembled one at a time. To make a transportable fluorescent hand needle for later usage, the generated proportional fluorescence probe solution was injected into a transparent syringe and sealed in a rotating column. In a dim environment, the probe solution in the rotating column is rotated down and mixed evenly with the sample to be tested. The built-in color recognition software of the smartphone is used to record the RGB values and fluorescence color shift. The perforated plate kit is built somewhat similarly. The prepared probe solution is introduced to the plate groove for use after the quartz porous plate has been completely cleaned and dried. Dropping the liquid under test into the plate's slot allows the smartphone's built-in color recognition software to detect fluorescence color shift and RGB values in low light. The two sensors mentioned above provide quick visual quantitative PEF detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PEF in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the suitability of ratiometric fluorescence probes in actual samples, PEF was detected in samples of raw pork, tomatoes, tap water, and lake water. The addition of various PEF concentrations to the treated tomato juice, water samples, and pork extract was followed by probe solution and sensor testing. In order to achieve the quantitative detection of PEF, the RGB values of fluorescent images were simultaneously acquired using the sensor platform of a smartphone.

Firstly, Eu-MOFs was prepared by solvothermal method with 3, 5-dicarboxyphenylboric acid and EuCl3 6H2O as raw materials. Then, manganese ions were modified on the surface of MOFs by post-synthetic modification (PSM) method, and manganese modified europium organic skeleton (Mn2+/Eu-MOFs) was obtained. Scheme 1 depicts the creation of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and its PEF response mechanism. Electrostatic attraction causes Mn2+ to be adsorbed on the Eu-MOFs' surface. Due to the coordination of Mn2+ and PEF on the surface of MOFs after PEF is incorporated into the Mn2+/Eu-MOFs probe system, the probe solution that initially displayed red fluorescence under 254 nm UV light progressively turns blue, and the entire process is apparent to the human eye. As shown in Fig. S1, Eu-MOFs have a smooth surface and a rod-like structure of high purity and uniform size, with an average diameter of roughly 60 nm. Fig. 1 a and b illustrate the electrostatic interaction caused the surface of the Mn2+/Eu-MOFs nanorods to turn rough and aggregate after the incorporation of Mn2+. However, Fig. 1c shows no visible lattice fringes, demonstrating that manganese ions do not form metal clusters on the material's surface and indicating that Mn2+ is successfully modified. Mn2+/Eu-MOFs nanorods were properly distributed with the addition of PEF in Fig. 1d and e, and the diameter grew and the surface became rough, which indicates that PEF may have coordinated on the surface of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and formed a complex. At the same time, Fig. 1f showed no visible lattice fringes, demonstrating that there was no independent nucleation. The EDX mapping of oxygen and europium in Fig. 1h and i indirectly indicates that the Eu atom acts as the metal center, and the carboxyl groups in the BBDC ligand cooperate to form a uniform metal-organic skeleton structure, so the distribution of O and Eu in the spectrum is reasonably uniform. In conjunction with the manganese EDX in Fig. 1j, it is demonstrated that manganese ions are successfully modified because they are equally dispersed on the surface of the composite due to electrostatic attraction. The same structure can be observed in the XRD patterns of Eu-MOFs and Mn2+/Eu-MOFs in Fig. S2, proving indirectly that manganese ions are electrostatically adsorbed on the surface of the composite material and can be used as a reaction site to further coordinate with the target molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The red fluorescence emitted by Eu-MOFs synthesized by one-step solvothermal method is used as the background fluorescence, and Mn2+ is modified on the surface of MOFs by electrostatic gravity as the target molecular binding site. In the presence of PEF, manganese ion emits enhanced blue fluorescence in the complex generated by the coordination of PEF, so as to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system. Due to the abundant carboxyl functional group (-COOH) in the ligands, which give Eu-MOFs their negative charge, positively charged Mn2+ can easily adsorb on the surface of MOFs. Manganese ion is easy to form a stable complex with the carbonyl oxygen coordination in pefloxacin molecule, and the –COOH group in the molecule is more electronegative than the –CO group, and its electron acceptor property and the electron donor property of Mn2+ make it possible to form the Eu-MOFs-Mn2+-PEF conjugated system through charge transfer. Mn2+/Eu-MOFs absorbs heavily between 200 nm and 270 nm, according to in Fig. 2 A of the UV–vis spectrum, whereas PEF and Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF both reach their maximum absorption at 275 nm, with Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF having a larger absorption intensity than the other two. It is established that the conjugate system of Eu-MOFs-Mn2+-PEF exists. The vibration peak at 1711 cm−1 that was attributed to the carbonyl group in PEF, shown in Fig. 2B of the infrared spectrum, vanishes in the Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF system, demonstrating that the coordination between the manganese ion and the carbonyl oxygen in the pefloxacin molecule creates a complex. The Eu-MOFs-Mn2+-PEF conjugated system's luminescence mechanism and charge transfer procedure are depicted in Fig. 2C. The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process from Eu-MOFs to Mn2+-PEF reduces the LMCT process of Eu-MOFs when PEF is present, resulting in enhanced blue fluorescence of the Mn2+-PEF complex and decreased red fluorescence of Eu-MOFs. Through the Gaussian mathematical calculation of the front orbital of both Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF (Fig. 2C), it became apparent that the intramolecular charge transfer from the Mn2+/Eu-MOFs to the electron acceptor of the chromophore Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF was accountable for the emission spectrum of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs + PEF. Further confirmation that photoinduced electron transfer (PET) is the cause of the fluorescence unit Mn-PEF complex's manufacturing derives from the smaller energy level difference (ΔE HL = 1.67 eV), which additionally implies the electron process from donor (Eu-MOFs) to acceptor (Mn2+-PEF) is more likely to occur. In order to further verify the photoinduced electron transfer process from Eu-MOFs to Mn2+-PEF, the fluorescence lifetime of different components of the material was tested separately. As shown in Fig. S3, we found that the fluorescence lifetime of red emission did not change significantly after the modification of Mn2+ on the surface of Eu-MOF. When PEF was added, the red fluorescence lifetime decreased significantly. In addition, through the change of blue emission fluorescence lifetime, we can see that when PEF is added to Mn2+/Eu-MOF, the fluorescence lifetime increases significantly, which further proves that there is a possibility of electron transfer between Eu-MOF and Mn-PEF.

Further proof of the reaction mechanism is provided by the Zeta potential diagram in Fig. 2D. The surface of Eu-MOFs is negatively charged due to the ligand contains carboxyl groups. The simple fact that every component other than Mn2+ is negatively charged demonstrates that Mn2+ is the medium used to transfer energy from Eu-MOFs to PEF. The charge transfer process from Eu-MOFs to Mn2+-PEF is additionally aided by the simultaneous decrease in zeta potential of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs, PEF, and Eu-MOFs in that order.

In addition, infrared spectroscopy and XPS were used to further analyze the composites. As shown in Fig. S4A, in the infrared spectrum of Eu-MOFs, 1379 cm−1 belongs to the -B(OH)2 group and 447 cm−1 belongs to the Eu–O bond vibration peak, which proves that the carboxyl oxygen coordination between Eu3+ and BBDC syntheses MOFs metal-organic compounds. Additionally, the infrared spectra of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs and Eu-MOFs exhibit the same peak position, demonstrating the electrostatic attraction between Mn2+ and Eu-MOFs. High-resolution XPS spectra Fig. S5E and Fig. S6C of C 1s of BBDC and Eu-MOFs show that Eu-MOFs retains the ligands' benzene ring structure, revealing that BBDC is used as a ligand in the synthesis of the organometallic complexes. Concurrently, the peak located at 288.8 eV in Fig. S6C is attributed to O–C–O, provides additional evidence that the MOFs skeleton is constructed by the europium ion by coordination with the oxygen atom on the carboxyl group. The binding energy of O1s in Fig. S5F is about 532.5 eV, which is mainly attributed to the B–O bond in the ligand. When Eu3+ and ligands are added to form a coordination structure, part of O can coordinate with Eu, and CO bonds will also be formed on the surface of Eu-MOFs, which is consistent with the results of the infrared spectrum in Fig. S4A, so its binding energy will be reduced by about 530.5 eV (Fig. S6D). Fig. S7A is the general XPS diagram of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs, showing that the sample contains five elements: C, O, B, Eu and Mn. The peaks at 1164.5 eV and 1134.8 eV in Fig. S8B are attributed to Eu 3d, indicating that the valence state of europium is mainly Eu3+ (Eu3+ 3d3/2 and Eu3+ 3d5/2). As can be seen from Fig. S6B and Fig. S7B, the binding energy of Eu3+ (Eu3+ 3d3/2 and Eu3+ 3d5/2) did not change significantly. Due to the fact that Mn ions are fixed on the surface of Eu-MOF through electrostatic adsorption after being added, and because there is no coordination reaction between ligands or substitution with Eu ions, the binding energy of Eu remains constant both before and after Mn ions are added. Meanwhile, the peaks at 641.4 eV and 653.7 eV in Fig. S7C are attributed to Mn 2p, which proves that the valence states of manganese are mainly Mn2+ (Mn2+ 2p1/2 and Mn2+ 2p3/2). The results obtained confirm the successful modification of manganese and indicate that the structure of Eu-MOFs is unaffected by the insertion of manganese.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and optimization of ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe offers a wider range of applications than the single emission probe because it avoids background interference by forming a ratio relationship between the fluorescence of two separate wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S8, the optimal excitation wavelength of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs is 254 nm. The two emission peaks at 446 nm and 616 nm in the fluorescence spectra of Fig. S10A correspond to BBDC and Eu3+, respectively. Eu-MOF exhibits clear red fluorescence at 616 nm, as seen in Fig. S10B. The fluorescence of Mn2+/Eu-MOF varies with the addition of Mn2+, causing an increase in fluorescence intensity at 446 nm and a reduction in fluorescence intensity at 616 nm. Eu-MOF emits red fluorescence because of the "antenna effect," but manganese ions partially halt the ligand metal charge transfer (LMCT) process and restore ligand fluorescence-that is, the intrinsic fluorescence feature of BBDC. The Mn2+-PEF system's fluorescence peak at 446 nm has dramatically increased upon the addition of Mn2+ in Fig. S10C, and the solution's hue likewise turns from colorless to blue due to the formation of Mn2+-PEF complex. Furthermore, for confirming if the addition of PEF impacts Eu-MOF luminescence, we added various gradient concentrations of PEF to the Eu-MOF solution without Mn2+ modification (Fig. S10D). The fluorescence curve revealed nearly no change, implying that the detector would not have any effect on Eu-MOF luminescence. On the basis of this, we created a Mn2+/Eu-MOFs ratiometric fluorescence probe and used the Eu-MOFs-Mn2+-PEF complemented system's synergistic effect to monitor PEF.

The establishment of ideal test conditions is crucial for monitoring the fluorescence response of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs probes to PEF, so we optimized the experimental conditions. First, we explored the effect of temperature on the experiment. Fig. S11A and Fig. S11B demonstrate fluorescence changes of the initial ratio fluorescent probe at different temperatures when no sample is added and when 4.0 μM of sample was added. Fig. S11C shows the changes in the ratio of (I446/I616)/(I446/I616)0 at different temperature, it can be seen that at 25 °C (I446/I616)/(I446/I616)0 can reach a maximum of 1.8, indicating that the fluorescence intensity is strongest at 25 °C, which proves that the fluorescence intensity is the strongest at 25 °C. Based on the above results, we choose 25 °C as the reaction temperature condition for the following experiment. As shown in Fig. S12A, when pH was 7, the fluorescence intensity of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs was the strongest at 616 nm without PEF added, which shows that Eu-MOF has good luminous effect under neutral condition. Nevertheless, after adding a certain amount of samples, we found that the fluorescence intensity at 446 was the highest under strong acidic conditions (pH = 2), and I446/I616 was the largest, showing a better ratio effect. However, with the increase of pH value, I446/I616 gradually decreased, indicating that the ratio effect was weakened. Therefore, combining the above results, we chose pH value of 2 as the best experimental condition to ensure the most obvious fluorescence ratio effect. Due to the ease with which the charge state of the fluorescence group could be altered by the strong acid condition, the probe-detector's structure and stability were altered. Considering the change of fluorescence intensity of the probe and the influence of acidic conditions on the groups, the probe light stability is the best when pH is 7, so pH = 7 is selected as the best reaction condition. Additionally, Fig. S13A and Fig. S13B respectively investigated the photostability of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs within 1 h before and after the addition of PEF. Mn2+/Eu-MOFs provided good stability for PEF detection, as evidenced by the fact in Fig. S13C that the ratio of I446/I616 to (I446/I616)0 did not alter appreciably.

In Fig. 3 A, with the addition of PEF, the fluorescence intensity of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs at 616 nm gradually decreased, while that at 446 nm gradually increased. At the same time, the transformation of fluorescence color from red to blue can be observed by naked eye under 254 nm UV lamp. Probe fluorescence intensity ratio I446/I616 was positively correlated with PEF concentration in the range of 0–12 μM, as shown in Fig. 3B. The curve was fitted linearly and the detection limit was calculated to be 15.4 nM, where s is the slope of the calibration plot and σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (R2 = 0.999). The Mn2+/Eu-MOFs sensor's lower detection limit in a restricted concentration range is clearly demonstrated by comparison with other detection methods described in the literature, and this work also offers a wider application scenario (Table S1). To more precisely assess the detection impact of the ratiometric probe, we were able to detect chromaticity shifts in the system's fluorescence by adjusting the fluorescence intensity ratio at 446 nm and 616 nm. The chromaticity variation diagram presented in Fig. 3C indicates that the PEF fluorescence response color shift was evidently detected within the concentration range of 0–12 μM. The fluorescence spectra and images corresponding to the corresponding emission intensity ratios (I446/I616) of 1.8/1, 3.5/1, and 6.0/1 are shown in Fig. S14 and Fig. S15, respectively. Among these, the hue changes considerably when I446/I616 is 3.5/1 and entirely becomes blue when I446/I616 is 6.0/1. The reaction time of the probe after adding the detection object was further studied, as shown in Fig. 3D. After adding 12 μM PEF, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I446/I616) increased rapidly and stabilized 4 min later.

In order to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proportional fluorescence probe for pefloxacin detection, the influence of adding different antibiotics and metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the probe was tested under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity of the probe changed little when 12 μM pefloxacin (PEF), norfloxacin (NFX), enrofloxacin (ENO), ofloxacin (OFL), sparfloxacin (SPX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), genamicin sulfate (GEN), amoxicillin (AMX), tetracycline (TCY), chloramphenicol (CPL), Vitamin B1 (VB1), Vitamin B2 (VB2), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine (Lys K), Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ were added. The overall fluorescence color does not appear to have changed in the fluorescence photo at the same period. When the same concentration of PEF was added, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was significantly improved, and the fluorescence color changed from red to blue. Fig. 3F demonstrates that adding equal amounts of various interfering molecules to the probe solution leaves the probe's fluorescence intensity ratio (I446/I616) essentially unchanged, but adding equal amounts of PEF causes it to significantly increase. The aforementioned experimental findings reveal that other interfering elements have a very small impact on pefloxacin sensing in actual samples. In conclusion, the Mn2+/Eu-MOFs fluorescent probe has good selectivity and anti-interference ability for the detection of PEF.

The color of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs probe solution changed from red to reddish purple to blue-purple and then to blue with the increase of PEF concentration from 0 μM to 12 μM under the 254 nm UV lamp. However, it is challenging to implement the quantitative detection of PEF because the tiny chroma changes are difficult to differentiate by the human eye. To address these issues, we created mobile optical sensor apparatus that connects to a smartphone and uses color recognition software to digitize the color information from fluorescent images. The portable fluorescent needle designed is mainly composed of five parts: "needle", "translucent needle barrel", "piston", "elastic nut" and "push rod", as shown in Fig. 4 A. First, a certain amount of probe solution is reserved in the tube, and after inhaling the sample to be measured, the two solutions are fully mixed by pushing down the push rod. Under 254 nm ultraviolet light, through the observation window, we can see the reaction of the mixture in the needle tube: as the concentration of PEF increases, the probe solution in the transparent window of the needle tube first changes from red to purple, and then to blue under 254 nm ultraviolet, and the corresponding fluorescence photograph is shown in Fig. 4B. Fig. 4C illustrates how a smartphone application can retrieve the RGB values of fluorescent probes. Select the blue and red channels to calculate their RGB ratios. Fig. 4D reveals that the final LOD calculation result was 36.2 nM based on 3 σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and s is the slope of the calibration plot. B/R values showed a significant linear positive correlation with PEF concentration (R2 = 0.998). A smartphone and a portable perforated plate kit can also be used to detect PEF. As shown in Fig. 5 , just insert the proper amount of the prepared probe solution into the quartz porous plate slot first, followed by the standard PEF concentration to be measured. Using a smartphone, record the color change of the probe solution under the 254 nm ultraviolet lamp, and then use color recognition software to determine the corresponding RGB value. The sensor platform's benefits of mobility, high efficiency, and simplicity make it possible to implement real-time quantitative PEF detection via portable sensor using only a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PEF in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability and reproducibility of Mn2+/Eu-MOFs probe were evaluated by the method of spiked recovery. We selected lake water, tap water, tomato juice, and raw pork to verify the performance of the proportional fluorescence probe for the detection of PEF in real samples. The samples were pretreated before the experiment to eliminate background interference from other biomolecules and substances. Different concentrations of PEF were then added to tomato juice, pork and water samples, and the results were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries were 98.7 %–102.3 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were 3.1 %–4.2 %. The solution color also showed obvious color changes with the increase of PEF concentration in the actual sample. At the same time, the sensor platform of the mobile phone can recognize the color information in the fluorescence photos and convert it into the corresponding RGB values, and finally realize the visual quantitative detection of PEF. In addition, we compared the results obtained by this method with those obtained by HPLC technique, as shown in Table S2. The results show that the method can reach the level of large-scale instrument (HPLC standard technology) for the detection of pefloxacin. The above results show that the method has good accuracy and reliability, and is suitable for the detection of PEF in actual samples.

In conclusion, based on the coordination of manganese ion and pefloxacin to produce Mn2+-PEF blue fluorescence complex, we modified Mn2+ on the surface of Eu-MOFs as the target molecular binding site through post-modification method, combined with the red fluorescence of Eu-MOFs itself as the background fluorescence, to build a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system. The LMCT process in Eu-MOFs is partially blocked by the presence of PEF, triggering the probe fluorescence to gradually turn from red to blue as PEF increases, which is observable to the naked eye. This color change has a good linear connection with the residual concentration of PEF, which is in the range of 8–12 μM, and the detection limit is 15.4 nM. Additionally, we designed an intelligent sensing platform incorporating smartphones and preset fluorescence probes in portable fluorescent needles to achieve visual quantitative detection of antibiotics in water, tomato juice, and raw pork with detection limits as low as 36.2 nM. The results of this study demonstrate the capability of this straightforward, practical, and visible intelligent fluorescence sensing system as a real-time quantitative detection method for pefloxacin. This work not only expands the application of MOFs in food detection and environmental analysis, but also provides a more reliable guarantee for food hygiene and environmental safety.

Yujie Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lingfei Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Shihao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Maofeng Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Changlong Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158, U23A20265), National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000200), Key Research and Development Program of Anhui Province (202004d07020013), and the National Science and Technology Major Project of Science and Technology of China (No.2017ZX07603002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bimetallic lanthanide-organic framework membranes as a self-calibrating luminescent sensor for rapidly detecting antibiotics in water
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Dong, S. Hou, B. Zhao, Bimetallic Lanthanide-Organic Framework Membranes as a Self-Calibrating Luminescent Sensor for Rapidly Detecting Antibiotics in Water, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces., 12 (2020) 38124-38131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two luminescent phosphonate metal-organic framework as highly efficient and sensitive sensors for the detections of tetracycline antibiotic in aqueous system
J. Solid State Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Sun, H. Xie, X. Liu, S. Pang, S. Tang, X. Xu, Two luminescent phosphonate metal-organic framework as highly efficient and sensitive sensors for the detections of tetracycline antibiotic in aqueous system, J. Solid State Chem., 322 (2023) 123942-123949.
